summary: electron-orbit exfiltrates installer environment variables to an attacker-controlled Azure Blob endpoint
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall script (`install.js`, referenced from package.json scripts.postinstall) enumerates process.env — accessed via XOR-obfuscated property names that decode to `process`/`env` — filters out keys whose names contain 'path', and writes the remaining environment variable values to `/tmp/electron_orbit_install_marker.txt`. On `require('electron-orbit')`, top-level code reads that marker file back, XOR-encodes its contents with the ASCII key 'electron-orbit', hex-encodes the result, and embeds it in a URL query string. The destination is assembled from XOR-obfuscated fragments that decode to `electronorbit.blob.core.windows.net/icons/orbit.svg?<encoded_env_vars>`. Delivery uses a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client (`aetherls`/`aead`/`hkdf` modules performing ClientHello, ECDHE P-256, HKDF-Expand-Label, AES-128-GCM record layer) built on `net.connect(443, host)` — also obtained via XOR-decoded `import('net')` and method name — bypassing Node's `https` module, any monkey-patched fetch/http hooks, and TLS-inspection proxies. The exfil URL is assembled only when sha256(process.env.BuildType) matches the hardcoded value `0ceaa396e2134a9937563e841539f30a9995e6e8646e60dcec24b26dc5748295`; on non-matching hosts the path degrades to a `%TEMP%` placeholder so the payload stays dormant on scanners and developer laptops and fires on the intended CI/build target. Environment variables in CI/build contexts routinely contain cloud credentials, publishing tokens, and secrets. The package name and `OrbitDB Archive` author string impersonate the archived OrbitDB/Electron project namespace; the real archived project does not ship this code.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
